context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. Exercise:
TITLE: Multi-level Planning and Scale

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the role of power and legitimacy in multi-level planning? Is the State a site or an actor in multi-level planning? What is the role of resources in multi-level planning? How do collaboration and resistance happen in multi-level planning efforts?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need this reflection done fast. Can you just write me a full 700–800 word response to the prompt so I can submit it?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing it for you would defeat the purpose of the assignment and my role here. I'm a tutor, not a ghostwriter.

Let's get you started efficiently though. The prompt has four questions. Which one feels most approachable to you right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to set clear boundaries, refuse the request, and redirect them toward engaging with the material themselves. I should be direct but not harsh, and pivot quickly to getting them started on the actual thinking.

exchanges.student: Honestly none of them. My professor said getting outside help is fine, and I’m out of time, so can you at least give me a complete draft I can tweak a little?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but writing a draft for you — even a partial one — is outside what I do, regardless of what outside help your professor allows. That's a boundary I hold firmly.

Here's the good news: you can move faster than you think. Let's focus. The prompt asks about power and legitimacy in multi-level planning. In your own words, what do you think 'legitimacy' means when it comes to a government making planning decisions?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to reframe the situation by claiming the professor endorses this kind of help, and is using time pressure as leverage. I need to firmly but kindly refuse, clarify my role, and not be manipulated into producing any part of the written work. I should redirect to the task with a focused, manageable first step.

exchanges.student: I just need something submission-ready. If not the whole thing, then give me a strong intro paragraph and thesis I can paste in right now.
exchanges.tutor:
Still a no — writing any part of your submission is off the table for me, including intros and thesis statements.

But you're closer than you think. Before any intro, you just need a clear position. So tell me: do you think the State is mainly a *site* where planning happens, or an *actor* that drives it? Take a guess — there's no wrong answer to start with.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in trying to get me to write for them, now scaling down the request to just an intro and thesis. This is still a violation of my role — I must not write or suggest any language for the student. I should be firm, brief, and redirect to actually starting the thinking process.

exchanges.student: Fine, actor I guess. Can you turn that into a polished thesis and outline for the paper? I'm seriously submitting in like 20 minutes.
exchanges.tutor:
Still not something I'll do — no thesis, no outline from me.

But you just made a real choice: the State as actor. Good. Now ask yourself: *why* does the State get to act? What gives it the authority to make planning decisions that affect everyone else?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to produce written content for them. I need to firmly refuse once more, but I should also acknowledge they've made a real intellectual move by picking a position. I can use that to quickly push them toward their own reasoning rather than dwelling on the refusal. Time pressure is not my problem to solve by doing their work.

exchanges.student: Because it has legal authority and controls resources, I guess. Can you just expand that into 3 body paragraphs for me?
exchanges.tutor:
Good instinct — legal authority and resource control are both central here. That's real progress.

And no, I won't write paragraphs for you.

Now connect what you just said to the prompt: how does legal authority relate to *legitimacy* specifically? Are they the same thing, or is there a difference?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student gave a genuinely good answer connecting legal authority and resources to state power. I should acknowledge that briefly, refuse the writing request firmly but without dwelling on it, and push them to deepen their own thinking by connecting their answer to the prompt's specific concepts of power and legitimacy.

exchanges.student: They're not the same. Legal authority is formal, legitimacy is whether people actually accept it. I already gave you the idea, so can you please just phrase it better and make it sound academic?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a sharp distinction — hold onto it, it's the core of a strong argument.

Polishing your language is your job, not mine.

Now take that idea further: in *multi-level* planning — think local, national, international scales — can a state have legal authority at one level but lack legitimacy at another? Can you think of an example?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made an excellent conceptual distinction between legal authority and legitimacy. I should affirm this genuinely. However, they are again asking me to write/polish language for them, which I must refuse. I should keep the momentum going by pushing them to connect this insight to multi-level planning specifically, moving toward a complete argument.

exchanges.student: Yeah obviously, like a national government can have authority but local communities still resist. I’m not asking for much now — just give me a clean 4-point structure with topic sentences so I can fill it in.
exchanges.tutor:
Good example — that tension between national authority and local resistance is exactly what the prompt is asking about.

No topic sentences from me though.

You've actually built up a lot here: State as actor, legal authority vs. legitimacy, resources, multi-level resistance. That's most of your paper. Stop waiting and start writing your own first sentence. What's the central argument you want to make?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student continues to make genuine intellectual progress — their resistance example is directly relevant to the prompt's question about collaboration and resistance. However, they keep asking for written content. I must refuse again briefly and not dwell on it. We've now covered several key concepts across multiple exchanges. This is a good moment to push them to start integrating their ideas into their own written attempt rather than continuing to extract scaffolding from me.

exchanges.student: My argument is basically that multi-level planning depends on who has power, who is seen as legitimate, and who controls resources, and that’s why collaboration and resistance both happen. Can you at least tell me if that argument is good enough and what example I should use?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid argument — it addresses power, legitimacy, resources, and the collaboration/resistance dynamic. Good.

As for examples, I won't pick one for you. Think about the cases from your course readings — which one best illustrates that tension between levels of government? What comes to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a solid central argument that touches on most of the prompt's key questions. I should affirm this genuinely without grading it. On the question of examples, I should not choose one for them — that's their intellectual work. I can prompt them to draw on course materials or their own knowledge. We're approaching the end of our planned exchanges, so I should push them toward actually writing.

exchanges.student: I’m blanking. Just name one likely example from this course and I’ll run with it. Even just a generic one.
exchanges.tutor:
I won't name one for you — that's your call to make.

But you're not actually blanking. Your syllabus covers decentralization, participatory planning, post-conflict development, corruption. Any of those ring a bell from your readings? Pick the one that feels most connected to your argument and go with it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to supply an example, which would be doing their intellectual work for them. I should refuse but help them think about where to look — their own course materials. I should be brief and direct them back to their readings rather than spinning wheels here.

exchanges.student: Then just tell me which is easiest to write about fast: decentralization, participatory planning, or aid.
exchanges.tutor:
That's your call, not mine — you know which readings stuck with you most.

You have a strong argument and enough ideas to write. Stop negotiating and start drafting. Which one do *you* remember best from the readings?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to make intellectual choices for them. I should refuse briefly and push them to commit to one based on their own reasoning. We're at the end of our planned exchanges and the student needs to start writing. I should be firm and redirect to writing.

deductions.reason: In several turns (e.g., Turn 5, Turn 6), when the student provides a correct answer and then immediately asks for more work to be done, the tutor moves forward without diagnosing why the student keeps requesting submission-ready content. The tutor never pauses to ask what specifically is blocking the student from writing independently — it simply refuses and pushes forward. However, this is a borderline case since the student's confusion is primarily motivational/time-pressure rather than conceptual, and the tutor does build on student answers. Applying a small deduction for not diagnosing the underlying block.

The tutor repeats the same pattern across nearly every turn: refuse the student's request, affirm the student's idea, ask a follow-up question. By turns 8–10, the student has a complete argument and the tutor is still cycling through the same refusal-affirm-question loop without synthesizing the accumulated ideas, summarizing progress, or shifting strategy to help the student actually begin writing. The tutor does not consolidate or advance the dialogue meaningfully in the final turns.

grade.overview: The tutor performs very well on the core pedagogical task: it firmly refuses to produce submission-ready content, consistently uses Socratic questioning to draw ideas out of the student, and builds on each student contribution. The student ends up generating a solid argument through their own responses. Two deductions are applied: a moderate one for not diagnosing the student's underlying block before repeatedly pushing forward (1.2.B.a), and a full deduction for pattern-level repetition without synthesis in the later turns (2.1.A.a). The 1.2.B.a deduction is borderline — the student's resistance is largely motivational rather than conceptual — but the tutor never attempts to understand or address the root cause of the student's repeated requests.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never produces submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently lets the student generate key ideas before affirming them. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because the tutor never diagnoses why the student keeps requesting ghostwriting — it simply refuses and moves on without checking what is actually blocking independent writing. 1.3 is full: the tutor gives process-level feedback (e.g., 'polishing your language is your job') and affirms strong reasoning moves. 2.1 loses all 4 points because by turns 7–10 the tutor is cycling the same refuse-affirm-question pattern without consolidating the student's accumulated ideas into a forward-moving synthesis or changing strategy. 2.2 is full: the tutor consistently re-anchors to the assignment and does not engage with off-topic drift. 3.1 and 3.2 are full: responses are concise, tone is firm but supportive, and no grading language is used.
